summary: Gruber examines Meta's decision to replace the native Windows WhatsApp app with a WebView2 web wrapper, reversing years of investment in a proper UWP app. He notes the irony of going from web wrapper to native and back to web wrapper, producing a bloated, sluggish experience with poor OS integration. He observes that the Windows reporter's complaints focus on performance and system integration rather than UI quality, which he attributes to differing platform priorities. Gruber then pivots to worry about whether this signals Meta's broader retreat from native desktop apps, potentially threatening the Mac Catalyst-based WhatsApp for Mac. He critiques the Mac app's many HIG violations while acknowledging it's still better than Electron alternatives. The piece ends with concern for non-American users who depend heavily on WhatsApp.
key_points: Meta reversed four years of native Windows app investment by replacing WhatsApp UWP with a WebView2 web wrapper that is bloated, slow, and poorly integrated with Windows features
The Windows reporter's complaints focused entirely on performance and OS integration, not UI quality — which Gruber reads as a platform-cultural difference between Windows and Mac users
WhatsApp for Mac, built with Catalyst, has numerous HIG violations but is still lighter and smoother than Electron alternatives like Signal and Beeper
The key question is whether Meta scrapped the native Windows app because they don't care about Windows specifically, or because they're retreating from native desktop apps entirely
Small details like chat list animations distinguish native apps from crude web wrappers, illustrating the quality gap Gruber consistently champions
money_quotes: It's bad enough that so many large companies never even bother creating native apps, but it feels even worse to see a good native app abandoned.

I suspect it's more that the Windows nerd mindset has UI design quality and adherence to recommended platform idioms way down on their list of priorities. That's why they're Windows users, not Mac users.
Has there ever once, in 41 years, been a good Mac app that puts a menu between 'File' and 'Edit'?
WhatsApp for Mac is a better Mac app than any Electron app I've ever used.
In the Signal and Beeper apps for Mac, an updated chat just zaps to the top of the chat list, with no animation at all. Gross.

The question is, did Meta scrap its native Windows app because they don't care that much about Windows in particular? Or because they don't care that much about native desktop apps, period — and a crude web app wrapper is coming to Mac next?
If you want an app for WatchOS, it has to be native. You can't ship a web app wrapper like an Electron app there.
key_insight: Meta's abandonment of a native Windows app for a web wrapper exemplifies the broader, dispiriting retreat from native desktop app quality — and may signal worse to come for Mac users who depend on WhatsApp.
